motion: This House Would ban facial recognition in public spaces

turns.content: Look, this stuff sounds futuristic and cool until you actually think about what it means day to day. Cameras tracking your face everywhere you go? That’s not security, that’s constant monitoring. And yeah people say “if you’re not doing anything wrong, who cares” but that’s not the point. The point is you shouldn’t have to justify existing in public without being tracked. Once this is everywhere, you don’t get that privacy back.

I hear the concern, but banning it outright ignores real benefits. Cities are struggling with crime, missing persons cases, even basic safety in crowded areas. Facial recognition, when used properly, can help solve crimes faster and prevent harm. The issue isn’t the technology itself, it’s how it’s governed. We shouldn’t throw out a useful tool because it could be misused.

But “used properly” is doing a lot of heavy lifting there. Who decides that? Governments? Companies? We’ve seen how that goes. These systems already have bias issues too, misidentifying people, especially minorities. So now you’ve got surveillance plus mistakes. That’s a bad combo. And again, once it’s normalized, it’s everywhere. You can’t opt out of public space.

Bias is a valid concern, but that’s an argument for improving the tech, not banning it. We don’t ban tools because early versions are flawed. Also, there are already regulations being developed around transparency and accountability. If anything, banning it pushes development underground or to less regulated places. Better to keep it visible and controlled.

This comes down to what kind of society you want. One where you can walk around anonymously, or one where every movement is logged. Even if it starts with good intentions, the long-term risk is too high. Some lines are worth drawing early.

I don’t think it’s that black and white. We already trade some privacy for safety in many areas of life. The goal should be balance, not extremes. With the right safeguards, this can be a net positive.

motion: This House Believes remote work is worse for productivity

turns.content: I’ve managed teams both ways, and honestly remote just doesn’t hit the same. Communication slows down, people take longer to respond, and collaboration turns into endless meetings. In office, you solve things in five minutes. Remote, it’s threads, pings, waiting. It adds friction everywhere.

I mean, that depends on how the team is set up. Remote done badly is bad, sure. But when it’s structured right, it’s actually more productive. People get fewer interruptions, no commute, more control over their time. A lot of people get way more done without office distractions.

But that assumes everyone is disciplined, and that’s just not reality. Some people thrive, sure, but a lot don’t. You lose visibility, accountability gets fuzzy, and team cohesion drops. That matters more than people think.

I’d argue that’s a management problem, not a remote problem. If you need to physically see someone to trust they’re working, that’s already an issue. Good systems track output, not presence. And honestly, a lot of office time isn’t productive either, it just looks like it.
